stack: Zero-build vanilla stack: plain HTML + one CSS file + native ES modules (`<script type="module">`), no framework, no bundler, no npm, no build step. In-browser ML via onnxruntime-web 1.19.2 (wasm execution provider) loaded lazily from jsDelivr CDN. Fonts from Google Fonts (Fraunces, Instrument Sans, IBM Plex Mono, display=swap). Canvas 2D for the attention heatmap (ch3) and loss-curve replay (ch8); everything else is DOM + CSS transitions. Python side (PyTorch ~958K-param char transformer, tools/ bake scripts) exists only offline to produce the ONNX model and baked JSON — the site itself is 100% static. Hosted on GitHub Pages from docs/.
fileStructure:
/Users/NavSha/Claude_Projects/taylor-transformer/
- DESIGN.md — the design-system source of truth (repo CLAUDE.md mandates reading it before any visual change)
- UPGRADE_PLAN.md — chapter plan, spike results, work-item log (all 14 done)
- docs/ — the deployed site (GitHub Pages root):
  - index.html — all 8 chapter shells + hero + sticky seed bar + footer (content lives in HTML, not JS)
  - studio.html — companion "songwriting copilot" page
  - .nojekyll, og.png (social card)
  - css/style.css — single stylesheet: tokens as :root CSS vars, then skeleton, then per-chapter sections
  - js/main.js — entry point: seed bar init + chapter registry + lazy IntersectionObserver boot
  - js/scroll.js — scrollytelling engine (initScrolly)
  - js/seed.js — shared seed state (pub/sub) + seed-bar UI
  - js/model.js — ONNX session/vocab loaders, forward(), encode/decode, displayChar
  - js/sampling.js — JS port of Python sample_next(): transformLogits + sampleNext + generate
  - js/bars.js — reusable probability-bar widget + softmax
  - js/chapters/ch1.js … ch8.js — one module per chapter, each exporting initCh{N}(panel) → optional step callback
  - js/studio.js — studio page logic
  - model/ — model.onnx (4.2 MB, logits+attention outputs), vocab.json, epochs.json (~1.5 KB), quiz.json (~1 KB), replay.json (~1.6 KB)
- tools/ — offline Python bakers: bake_epoch_samples.py (ch5), build_quiz_bank.py (ch6), bake_training_replay.py (ch8)
- spikes/ — feasibility spikes (ONNX export/parity, attention export, browser training, sampling parity)
- model.py / train.py / generate.py / checkpoints/ — the offline PyTorch project
designSystem: "Editorial midnight" — dark-only, warm. All tokens are :root CSS custom properties in docs/css/style.css, spec'd in DESIGN.md. Colors: ground #120E0B (warm near-black), surface #1A1512, surface-2 #221C17, border #2C251E, text #F0EAE2, muted #A89C8E, primary accent --machine amber #E4A94F (+ dim #8A6A35), secondary --rose #B4637A, semantic success #7FA95C / error #C4564A / info #8A96C4. Attention heat ramp: ground → machine-dim → machine → rose. Fonts: Fraunces (serif display, weight ~330–400, italic accent word colored amber in headings), Instrument Sans (body/UI), IBM Plex Mono (machine voice, kickers, data). THE signature rule ("Two Voices"): anything the model reads or writes is IBM Plex Mono in amber; everything the author says is serif/sans in warm white — a teaching device baked into typography. Layout: 680px reading column, 1060px page max, 8px spacing base, 96px+ between chapters, radius 4/8/12px (never pill), panels = surface + 1px border + radius 12 + shadow 0 20px 60px rgba(0,0,0,.45). Texture: a single fixed-position SVG feTurbulence paper-grain overlay (body::before, opacity .35). Motion: ink-glow text-shadow (0 0 14px rgba(228,169,79,.65)) on the freshest typed char, blinking block cursor, bar widths animate via CSS transition (150–250ms ease-out, update-in-place), step reveals 400ms ease-out translateY(12px); full prefers-reduced-motion handling (instant typing, no cursor blink, replay jumps to end). Chapter markers: mono 12px uppercase letter-spacing .18em, chapter number in rose, step numbers ("00 / a grid") in muted.
widgetArchitecture:
One JS module per chapter (docs/js/chapters/ch{1..8}.js), each exporting async initCh{N}(panel) that: (1) fetches its data, (2) replaces the panel's placeholder with innerHTML template, (3) wires events, (4) returns an optional (stepIndex) => {} callback that the scrolly engine drives. main.js holds a registry {sectionId → {panelId, load: () => import('./chapters/chN.js')}} and boots each chapter lazily via dynamic import() when its section comes within 600px of the viewport (IntersectionObserver, rootMargin '600px 0px') — so the 4.2 MB model download starts around chapter 1, not page load, and visitors who never scroll download nothing. Step events fired before boot are buffered in pendingSteps and replayed. Boot failures degrade gracefully (placeholder shows "this demo could not load — <msg>").

HOW THE MODEL RUNS: real inference in-browser via ONNX Runtime Web — NOT precomputed for chapters 1–4, 7, and the Studio. model.js lazily injects ort.min.js from CDN, creates one InferenceSession over model/model.onnx (wasm EP), memoized in a module-level promise. The ONNX export (spikes/spike3_attention_export.py) outputs BOTH logits (1,T,V) and full per-layer/per-head post-softmax attention (layers,B,heads,T,T) from a single forward — attention costs nothing when unused. forward(ids) slides a 256-token window, feeds int64 tensor "tokens", returns last-position logits + attention. Sampling (temperature → top-k → top-p → rep-penalty) is a line-for-line JS port of the Python sample_next(), parity-verified to ~2e-7; transformLogits() is exposed separately from sampleNext() so sliders can reshape the displayed distribution without sampling. Vocab is a tiny char_to_idx/idx_to_char JSON.

HYBRID with precomputed JSON where live inference is impossible or risky: ch5 (epoch comparison) and ch6 (quiz) and ch8 (training replay) fetch tiny baked JSON files (epochs.json: seed→{e10,e30,best} text; quiz.json: [{t, src, mem}]; replay.json: {curve: [[epoch,loss]…], samples: [{epoch,loss,seed,text}]}) generated offline by audited Python scripts in tools/ — every baked sample is profanity-screened and memorization-audited (longest verbatim corpus run < 30 chars, metrics-only printing).

Shared state: seed.js is a tiny pub/sub store — one seed phrase set by the sticky seed bar flows through every chapter (onSeedChange), input filtered to the model's 91-char vocab. Shared UI: bars.js builds probability-bar rows once and updates widths in place (CSS transitions, no re-mount). Every async chapter uses a generation-counter token to cancel stale in-flight runs when the seed changes or a new action starts.
reusableForMlPapers: The whole zero-build architecture: docs/ folder on main + GitHub Pages + .nojekyll + native ES modules + dynamic import() — no npm, no CI, git push = deploy. Copy docs/js/main.js's chapter registry + lazy IntersectionObserver boot (rootMargin '600px', pendingSteps buffering, graceful per-chapter failure message) verbatim.

docs/js/scroll.js (50 lines) — a complete framework-free scrollytelling engine: .scrolly > .steps/.sticky markup, closest-to-viewport-center step activation, per-chapter step callback. Directly reusable for a papers timeline (each paper = a chapter, each key idea = a step driving a sticky visual).
The chapter module contract: async initChN(panel) → returns (stepIndex)=>{} callback. Keeps every widget self-contained, lazily loaded, and scroll-driven with one uniform interface.

The CSS token architecture in docs/css/style.css: all design tokens as :root vars, one file, sections ordered tokens → skeleton → per-chapter. Plus specific crafts worth lifting: SVG feTurbulence grain overlay on body::before, panel recipe (surface/border/radius-12/deep shadow), .kicker/.chapter-label mono-uppercase marker style, clamp()-based display type, and the prefers-reduced-motion block.

The 'Two Voices' signature rule pattern — one typeface+color with a hard semantic meaning (here amber mono = the machine speaking). For ml-papers, an equivalent could be: mono+accent = quotes from the actual papers / model outputs, serif = the narrator. It's the single strongest identity device on the site and costs nothing.

The persistent sticky 'seed bar' + seed.js pub/sub store: one user-supplied input that flows through every chapter and re-runs every widget. For ml-papers this maps to e.g. one example sentence/image/prompt threading through paper demos.

Precomputed-JSON widget pattern (ch5/ch6/ch8): offline Python bakers in tools/ that emit tiny audited JSON into docs/model/, consumed by fetch(). For ml-papers, most 'run the model' moments (training curves, attention maps from papers, benchmark tables, sample outputs across model scales) should be baked JSON like this — only reach for live ONNX inference where interactivity truly needs it.

The ch8 training-replay widget (docs/js/chapters/ch8.js): requestAnimationFrame playback of a real loss curve on canvas synced with checkpoint samples ('watch it learn' without live training). Perfect template for AlexNet/scaling-laws/RLHF training-story chapters; replay.json shape ({curve, samples}) is generic.

The break-it/failure chapter pattern (ch7): preset impossible-prompt chips + free input + live running-confidence meter + moralizing panel-note ('confidence is not correctness'). Every good ML-paper explainer needs an honest failure chapter; this is a proven layout for it.

The quiz pattern (ch6): 10-round human-or-machine game from a tiny JSON bank with a planted 'gotcha' item that teaches a concept (memorization) and score-dependent verdict copy. Great engagement beat mid-scroll for ml-papers (e.g. 'which model wrote this?', 'which paper is older?').
bars.js update-in-place pattern: build DOM rows once, mutate widths/text so CSS transitions animate — never re-mount. Applies to any live chart in the new site.

Generation-counter cancellation idiom (const gen = ++generation; … if (gen !== generation) return;) used in every async widget — the simplest correct way to handle stale async work when inputs change.

If any ml-papers demo runs a real model: the ONNX Runtime Web recipe — lazy CDN script inject, single memoized wasm session, int64 token tensor, export extra tensors (attention) as secondary outputs, keep the model ≤ ~5 MB checked into docs/model/. Parity-test JS sampling against Python first (spikes/sampling-parity.html approach).

Site-craft checklist from item 13: og.png social card + full OG/twitter meta, theme-color matching the ground color, footer cross-linking sibling explainers, README section pointing at the live site, aria-pressed on toggle chips, aria-live on typed output.

DESIGN.md as an enforced artifact: repo CLAUDE.md says 'always read DESIGN.md before any visual decision; in QA mode flag code that doesn't match it'. Create the sibling DESIGN.md for ml-papers first (a /design-consultation output) — this repo shows the payoff: style.css literally cites it as source of truth.

Sibling-site identity system: dogs-vs-cats is the cool-blue 'machine learns to see', this is warm-amber 'machine learns to write' — same structural DNA (dark scrollytelling, sticky panels, chapter markers), deliberately contrasted palette/typeface. ml-papers should be designed as the third sibling: shared skeleton, its own temperature and serif/mono voices.
scrollytellingApproach: Framework-free, ~50 lines (docs/js/scroll.js). Markup contract: each chapter section contains a .scrolly grid with .steps (a column of .step blocks, each min-height 70vh) and a .sticky panel (position: sticky; top: 96px) holding the interactive visual. Engine: one IntersectionObserver per .scrolly (rootMargin '-30% 0px -30% 0px', thresholds 0/.25/.5/.75/1); among intersecting steps it activates the one whose center is closest to the viewport center, toggles .is-active (CSS: inactive steps at opacity .25 translateY(12px), active fades/slides in over 400ms), and calls the chapter's onStep(stepIndex) callback so the sticky panel swaps state (ch1 reveals alphabet/IDs via panel classes, ch3 switches attention layers, ch4 snaps slider presets, ch5 switches epoch tabs, ch2 triggers the q→u demo on step 3). Chapters 6–8 skip the scrolly grid and use a single-column .prose layout because they're games/finales, not step sequences. Mobile (<780px): grid collapses to one column and the sticky panel docks under the seed bar (top: 52px) above the scrolling text. A second, coarser IntersectionObserver in main.js (rootMargin 600px) handles lazy chapter boot separately from step activation.
failureChapterPattern: Chapter 7 "Break it" (docs/js/chapters/ch7.js + the .prose copy in index.html). Recipe: (1) preset chip buttons of prompts the model never trained on ('Photosynthesis requires', 'function add(a, b)', '2 + 2 =') plus a free-text input (debounced 400ms); (2) the model continues the prompt LIVE, typed char-by-char at 35ms/char with the seed part dimmed and the fresh char glowing; (3) the twist instrument — a live confidence meter: mean probability the model assigned to each character it chose, rendered as an amber fill bar + percentage that climbs while it confidently writes nonsense; (4) an adaptive panel-note punchline: if the user's prompt contained out-of-vocab characters it lists them ('It couldn't even read "🌱" — not in its 91-character alphabet'), otherwise 'Fluent. Confident. And it has no idea what any of those words mean.'; (5) closing prose generalizes the lesson to frontier AI ('Confidence is not correctness… the same is true, at a million times the scale, of every AI you use'). Safety details: unknown chars stripped before encoding, a ROUGH profanity regex bails generation, and a generation counter cancels stale runs. The memorization variant of 'failure' lives in ch6 as the quiz gotcha (mem:true items that are verbatim corpus lines). The pattern mirrors the sibling dogs-vs-cats chapter 5 — one honest chapter per explainer is a deliberate house convention.
deploySetup: GitHub Pages, classic "deploy from a branch" mode: the docs/ folder on main of github.com/NavSha/taylor-transformer serves https://navsha.github.io/taylor-transformer/. No GitHub Actions, no build — git push to main is the whole pipeline. docs/.nojekyll (empty file) disables Jekyll processing. The 4.2 MB model.onnx and all baked JSON are committed directly into docs/model/. onnxruntime-web comes from the jsDelivr CDN at runtime (preconnect hints in <head>), so nothing is bundled. All asset URLs are relative (model/…, css/…, js/…), which makes the project-subpath hosting work. Local dev = any static server (python3 -m http.server) from repo root or docs/. Offline artifacts (checkpoints/*.pt, data/, runs/, spikes/out/) are gitignored; only vocab.json from checkpoints is kept.
gotchas: ONNX Runtime Web is inference-only — the planned live in-browser training finale was cut and replaced with a replay of the real training run (loss curve from TensorBoard events + per-checkpoint baked samples). If ml-papers wants live training, the tfjs spike found: batched-matmul gradients need flattening (B,T,D)x(D,V) to 2D, the wasm backend lacks the gather gradient (use one-hot matmul for embedding lookup), and you must tf.nextFrame() every step or the tab hangs.

One wasm InferenceSession = one queue: concurrent session.run() calls contend rather than parallelize. The Studio generates its three suggestions sequentially and streams each in as it lands (docs/js/studio.js comment).

Every async widget needs stale-run cancellation — the repo's idiom is a module-level generation counter bumped on each new run/seed change, checked after every await. Without it, seed changes interleave two typing runs.

Content liability for generative output shown unprompted: every baked sample AND live unprompted continuations (ch3, ch7, studio) pass a profanity regex, and baked samples pass a memorization audit (longest verbatim corpus run < 30 chars, script prints metrics only, never matched text). For ml-papers, the analog is checking quoted-figure licensing / not shipping copyrighted paper text.
GitHub Pages from docs/ requires .nojekyll, relative URLs (site lives under /taylor-transformer/), and tolerance for a multi-MB binary in git — fine at 4.2 MB, would not scale to a 100 MB model.
int64 model inputs need BigInt64Array in JS (BigInt64Array.from(window.map(BigInt))) — a classic onnxruntime-web stumbling block.
The mobile scrolly inversion matters: below 780px the sticky panel moves ABOVE the steps and sticks under the seed bar, otherwise the visual is never visible while reading steps.

Model download cost is hidden by lazy boot: ort.min.js and model.onnx only load when chapter 1 approaches (600px rootMargin), and loadVocab/loadModel are separately memoized so cheap chapters (ch1 needs only vocab.json) don't pull the model.
Attention export trick: emitting per-layer/per-head attention as a second ONNX output is free when unused, letting one 4.2 MB model serve every chapter — no separate 'attention model'.

IntersectionObserver step selection must pick the intersecting step closest to viewport center (not just any intersecting one) or short steps double-activate; the -30%/-30% rootMargin band is the tuned value.
prefers-reduced-motion needs real handling in JS, not just CSS: ch8 jumps straight to the final replay state, typing renders instantly.

vocab-filter user input at the source (seed.js strips out-of-vocab chars on set) so downstream widgets never see unencodable text — except ch7, which deliberately keeps the dropped chars to teach the lesson.
